title: Student CRM & Engagement Analytics
subtitle: Predicting Retention Risk Through Service Usage Patterns
description: Interactive analytics dashboard applying customer relationship management (CRM) techniques to student retention and engagement prediction. Analyzes service adoption patterns, commitment levels, and communication channels as predictors of student persistence.
keyInsight: Service adoption strongly predicts student persistence—students using 4+ support services show <5% dropout rates, while those using 0-1 services show 40%+ dropout rates.

dataSource.name: IBM Telco Customer Churn Dataset
dataSource.description: Publicly available telecommunications customer churn data, translated and adapted to student engagement and retention context

dashboardTabs.name: Executive Summary
dashboardTabs.description: Engagement distribution, risk band validation, and lifecycle vs. engagement patterns

dashboardTabs.name: Attrition Risk
dashboardTabs.description: Risk score distribution, calibration, and service usage by risk band

dashboardTabs.name: Service Usage
dashboardTabs.description: Service adoption rates, engagement correlation with dropout, and usage patterns

dashboardTabs.name: Billing & Commitment
dashboardTabs.description: Dropout by commitment type and payment method; cost vs. engagement analysis

dashboardTabs.name: Profile Drilldown
dashboardTabs.description: Targeted segment analysis with filtering; high-risk and low-risk profile comparison

keyFindings: Service adoption is the strongest predictor of student persistence—engagement dominates demographic factors
Students with annual commitment contracts show 25-30% lower dropout than open enrollment students
Communication channel diversity correlates with engagement—multi-channel users show lower risk
New students (first 3-6 months) show highest dropout risk; early engagement interventions likely high-leverage
Open enrollment students represent disproportionate dropout volume despite good early-stage characteristics
Services show intercorrelation in adoption—students who adopt one support service tend to adopt others
practicalApplications: Early warning system for at-risk student identification
Triage for proactive student outreach and intervention targeting
Service awareness and utilization optimization planning
Program-level retention analysis and improvement evaluation
Intervention ROI modeling for institutional leadership
limitations: Data source is telecommunications churn, not actual student data—represents one plausible engagement framing
Supports correlation analysis but not causal inference—cannot determine if services cause persistence or persistence causes service adoption
Risk scoring based on historical customer behavior; current student engagement patterns may differ significantly
Does not account for external macro factors (economic conditions, family emergencies, pandemic impacts)
Masks institutional and program-level variation in retention patterns
ethicalConsiderations: Risk predictions are probabilistic tendency, not deterministic—many high-risk students persist
Avoid self-fulfilling prophecy: use risk score to increase support, not justify withdrawal
Model incorporates demographics; disaggregate analysis to identify and mitigate demographic disparities
Intervention effectiveness not proven from this data; use model to identify candidates for experimental evaluation
interactiveElements: Responsive scatter plots with hover tooltips
Risk band filtering in profile drilldown
targetAudience: Student Services Leadership (VP of Student Success, Dean of Students)
Chief Analytics Officers / Strategic Planning Leadership
projectNotes: This project demonstrates the application of CRM analytics frameworks to educational contexts. While based on adapted commercial data rather than actual student records, it illustrates how retention analytics, service-engagement modeling, and predictive segmentation can transform institutional understanding of persistence drivers and enable data-driven retention strategy. The model shows engagement as the central variable linking student characteristics, institutional services, and persistence outcomes.
